sections.biography-source-undefined:
Zhu Huan was an important general of Wu who served Sun Quan as early as 200. Huan was known for being a man of the people and generous. Around 200, he encountered an epidemic, and grain was scarce and expensive. Medicine was hard to come across and expensive. Huan saw this, and so he divided up the government’s supply and passed it along. For this, he won the people’s hearts. Huan commonly fought bandits in his early military career, and would once again rise to defend Danyang and Poyang against mountain bandits. The city did not have walls, and the magistrate was killed by the bandits. In response, Zhu Huan supervised various officers to punish them and in the end, all was recaptured.
Later, Zhu Huan would replace Zhou Tai as Commander of Ruxu and in 222, would defend against Wei’s Commander in Chief, Cao Ren, in an impressive display of courage and great leadership. Cao Ren proclaimed he would move east and invade Xianqi. Rumors spread and Huan heard of this, so he acted accordingly and divided his troops to defend. However, this was a ruse, and after his troops left, Cao Ren advanced with his main force towards Ruxu. Immediately, when Huan learned of this, he sent a messenger to recall his detachment back to Ruxu, but before they could arrive Cao Ren had reached Ruxu. Zhu Huan was severely outnumbered and amounted to no more than 5,000 troops, while Cao Ren attacked with several tens of thousands of troops. This caused great panic among everyone. Zhu Huan excellently responded with a rally speech. Huan came up with a plan to feign weakness to entrap Cao Ren. This would prove successful, as Cao Ren would send his son, Cao Tai along with general Chang Diao, Wang Shuang, and Zhuge Qian. Zhu Han resisted Cao Tai who set fire to their camp and withdrew, and Chang Diao was slain while Wang Shuang was captured. It is said Huan’s army of 5,000 killed and drowned more than 10,000 soldiers. When Sun Quan heard this, he was overjoyed by Huan’s accomplishments. Once again, Zhu Huan was promoted to General Who Exherts Prowess and received the Marquis of Jiaxing.
In 229, Zhu Huan was given the rank of General of the Front and Governship of Qingzhou. He also received Jie (the ability to execute officers under martial law without having to send them to the capital). By 237, Huan’s reputation had become well known by Wei, as described in one battle where the Grand Administrator of Wei, Li Yong, wished to cross the river and strike, however once he saw Zhu Huan hiding in the rear, he did not dare cross.
The most controversial part of Zhu Huan’s military career would be when Huan slew his own officer’s servants, Quan Cong, who ranked above him and commanded more troops than him. Huan felt he had deserved to be of higher rank and was known to be hot-headed. A scuffle arose after he wished to speak to Cong about the matter, but his servants would not take him to Cong and so Huan lashed out and slew some servants of Cong’s. Huan immediately turned himself over and pleaded that he was insane. Quan remembered his earlier achievements and pitied him and thus did not charge him with a crime. He was deemed to be “sick” and was temporarily exiled for a few months until he was cured. Some called Zhu Huan a traitor for this act and did not forgive him. During this time of exile, his son, Zhu Yi, would command his troops. After a few months had passed, Zhu Huan returned to his post.
The relationship between Sun Quan and Zhu Huan was such that in one event soon after, Sun Quan was enjoying wine with Zhu Huan when Huan said to Quan, “Your minister is now to go far away, but first he would like to stroke the beard of his lord.” Zhu Huan advanced and stroked Quan’s beard, and Huan said, “Now I can actually say I have stroked the tiger’s beard!” Sun Quan laughed at this.
Huan by nature valued being in front and on the top. If he was restrained, he would become enraged. Yet Huan thought little of wealth and valued righteousness and had a strong memory. When he met someone once, for decades he would not forget him, and among his division of retainers of ten thousand people, he completely remembered all of them and their wives and children. He cared for officials and soldiers and was known to be kind to them and share his wealth with his troops and their parents. Around 238, Huan became seriously ill and the whole camp was worried and grieved. At 62, Zhu Huan passed away. Zhu Huan had died poor as a result of constantly sharing his wealth among his soldiers and family, so Sun Quan provided for the funeral. Zhu Yi would succeed Zhu Huan and continue on Huan’s legacy and become just as famed a general.

full_text:
Zhu Huan (Xiumu) 朱桓 (休穆)
ARomance of the Three KingdomsEncyclopedia officer profile.
Zhu Huan (朱桓), styledXiumu (休穆), was born in Wu. Hot-headed, yet also kind. Murdered the servants of Quan Cong. Destroyed Cao Ren at Ruxu. Zhu Huan was affiliated with the Wu Kingdom. Zhu Huan lived during the Later Han Dynasty and Three Kingdoms period of Chinese history.
Return to theThree Kingdoms Encyclopediaor browse theEncyclopedia Directory.
